subject: Databases
title: Buffer Pool Tuning
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Buffer Pool Tuning.

hook.text: Postgres defaults to 128 MB shared buffers. On a server with 64 GB RAM, that's painfully small.

buildup.text: shared_buffers should be about 25% of total RAM. That gives Postgres a large in-memory cache.

discovery.text: effective_cache_size tells the planner how much RAM is available (OS cache + shared_buffers). Set it to 75% of RAM.

twist.text: Setting shared_buffers too high steals memory from the OS file cache. Both caches matter.

climax.text: InnoDB's buffer pool size has similar tuning. Set innodb_buffer_pool_size to 70–80% of RAM on a dedicated MySQL server.

punchline.text: Tune the buffer pool first. It's the single biggest performance knob.

quiz.question: Why shouldn't you set shared_buffers to 100% of available RAM?
options: The operating system also needs memory for its file cache, which databases rely on
PostgreSQL can only use a maximum of 1 GB
It would cause the database to restart constantly
